annotations.label: Division terminology

anchor.snippet: What is being divided is called the dividend

annotations.note: Wikipedia-style naming of dividend/divisor/quotient is informal and not encoded as a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Euclidean division of natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The division with remainder or Euclidean division of two natural numbers provides an integer quotient

annotations.note: `Nat.div_add_mod` (with `Nat.mod_lt`) gives the quotient-remainder decomposition for natural numbers.

annotations.label: Division as inverse of multiplication

anchor.snippet: division is the inverse operation to multiplication

annotations.note: In any `DivInvMonoid`, `div_eq_mul_inv` states `a / b = a * b⁻¹`, formalizing division as multiplication by an inverse.

annotations.label: 21 apples divided by 4

anchor.snippet: if 21 apples are divided between 4 people, everyone receives 5 apples again, and 1 apple remains

annotations.note: A pedagogical numerical example, not a theorem of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean domain

anchor.snippet: Those in which a Euclidean division (with remainder) is defined are called Euclidean domains

annotations.note: `EuclideanDomain` is a Mathlib typeclass capturing rings equipped with a Euclidean division (quotient + remainder with a well-founded size function).

annotations.label: Polynomial ring as Euclidean domain

anchor.snippet: polynomial rings in one indeterminate

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a `EuclideanDomain` instance on `R[X]` (`Polynomial R`) when `R` is a field, formalizing the Euclidean-division structure on univariate polynomial rings.

annotations.label: Field and division ring

anchor.snippet: Those in which a division (with a single result) by all nonzero elements is defined are called fields and division rings

annotations.note: `DivisionRing` (and its commutative variant `Field`) formalize structures in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse, so division by nonzero elements is total.

annotations.label: Units in a ring

anchor.snippet: In a ring the elements by which division is always possible are called the units

annotations.note: `IsUnit` (and the type `αˣ` of units) formalize the invertible elements of a monoid/ring — those by which division is unambiguously possible.

annotations.label: Quotient group

anchor.snippet: Another generalization of division to algebraic structures is the quotient group

annotations.note: `QuotientGroup.Quotient.group` provides the `Group (G ⧸ N)` instance for a normal subgroup, formalizing the quotient group construction.

annotations.label: Quotition and partition

anchor.snippet: The simplest way of viewing division is in terms of quotition and partition

annotations.note: Quotitive vs partitive interpretations of division are pedagogical concepts not modelled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 20 apples in five groups

anchor.snippet: 20 apples divide into five groups of four apples

annotations.note: Illustrative arithmetic example, not a Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Division is non-commutative

anchor.snippet: division is not commutative

annotations.note: Mathlib has no explicit lemma asserting the non-commutativity of division on a concrete type.

annotations.label: Division is non-associative

anchor.snippet: Division is also not, in general, associative

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration explicitly states the non-associativity of `/`.

annotations.label: Non-associativity example
annotations.note: A numerical witness to non-associativity, not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Left-associativity convention

anchor.snippet: Division is traditionally considered as left-associative

annotations.note: Left-associativity of `/` is a Lean parsing convention (the `/` notation), not a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Right-distributivity over addition/subtraction

anchor.snippet: Division is right-distributive over addition and subtraction

annotations.note: `add_div : (a + b) / c = a / c + b / c` formalizes right-distributivity of division in a field (with `sub_div` for subtraction).

annotations.label: Division is not left-distributive

anchor.snippet: However, division is not left-distributive

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma explicitly states that `/` fails to be left-distributive.

annotations.label: Multiplicative inverse method

anchor.snippet: a division by x may be computed as the product by the multiplicative inverse of x

annotations.note: `div_eq_mul_inv` directly states the computation of `/` via multiplication by an inverse.

annotations.label: Modular inverses for prime modulus

anchor.snippet: In modular arithmetic (modulo a prime number) and for real numbers , nonzero numbers have a multiplicative inverse

annotations.note: `ZMod.instField` provides a `Field` instance on `ZMod p` for prime `p`, giving multiplicative inverses for every nonzero element.

annotations.label: Euclidean division of integers

anchor.snippet: given two integers, a , the dividend , and b , the divisor , such that b ≠ 0, there are unique integers q , the quotient , and r , the remainder

annotations.note: The Euclidean domain instance on `ℤ` together with `EuclideanDomain.quotient_mul_add_remainder_eq` packages existence and uniqueness of integer Euclidean division.

annotations.label: Integers not closed under division

anchor.snippet: Integers are not closed under division

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states this informal closure failure; `Int.div` is in fact total via Euclidean truncation.

annotations.label: Division of rational numbers

anchor.snippet: The division of two rational numbers p / q and r / s can be computed as

annotations.note: The `Field ℚ` instance provides division on `ℚ` satisfying `div_eq_mul_inv` and the usual field laws.

annotations.label: Division of real numbers

anchor.snippet: It is defined such that a / b = c if and only if a = cb and b ≠ 0

annotations.note: The `Field ℝ` instance defines `/` on `ℝ`, with `div_eq_iff` providing the characterization `a / b = c ↔ a = c * b` when `b ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Division of complex numbers

anchor.snippet: Dividing two complex numbers (when the divisor is nonzero) results in another complex number, which is found using the conjugate of the denominator

annotations.note: `Complex.inv_def` expresses `z⁻¹ = conj z * (normSq z)⁻¹`, which combined with `div_eq_mul_inv` yields the conjugate-of-denominator formula.

annotations.label: Complex division in polar form

anchor.snippet: Division for complex numbers expressed in polar form is simpler than the definition above

annotations.note: Polar coordinates and the exponential form exist via `Complex.polarCoord` and `Complex.exp_log`, but no dedicated lemma packages division as quotient of moduli with subtracted arguments.

annotations.label: Polynomial division

anchor.snippet: One can define the division operation for polynomials in one variable over a field

annotations.note: `Polynomial.div` (with `Polynomial.divByMonic`) defines division of polynomials over a field, giving a Euclidean domain instance on `R[X]`.

annotations.label: Matrix division

anchor.snippet: The usual way to do this is to define A / B = AB −1

annotations.note: Mathlib has matrix inversion `Matrix.inv` and `Matrix.mul`, so `A * B⁻¹` is expressible, but no dedicated `Div` instance / `A / B` notation for matrices is provided.

annotations.label: Left and right matrix division

anchor.snippet: one can also define a left division or so-called backslash-division as A \ B = A −1 B

annotations.note: Mathlib does not provide backslash left-division `A \ B` on matrices as a named operation.

annotations.label: Identities for left/right matrix division

anchor.snippet: However, it holds that A / ( BC ) = ( A / C ) / B and ( AB ) \ C = B \ ( A \ C )

annotations.note: Since matrix `/` and `\` are not defined in Mathlib, these identities are not stated.

annotations.label: Pseudoinverse division

anchor.snippet: division can also be defined as multiplication by the pseudoinverse

annotations.note: The Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse is not currently formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Left/right division in a magma

anchor.snippet: left division of b by a (written a \ b ) is typically defined as the solution x to the equation a ∗ x = b

annotations.note: Mathlib has the `Div` typeclass and uses `a / b = a * b⁻¹` in groups, but does not define abstract magma left/right division as the solution to `a * x = b` (no `Quasigroup` class).

annotations.label: Quasigroup

anchor.snippet: A magma for which both a \ b and b / a exist and are unique for all a and all b

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `Quasigroup` (or `Loop`) typeclass.

annotations.label: Finite ring with cancellative elements

anchor.snippet: If a ring is finite and every nonzero element is cancellative, then by an application of the pigeonhole principle , every nonzero element of the ring is invertible

annotations.note: `Finite.isField_of_domain` shows any finite integral domain is a field, exactly the pigeonhole-principle statement.

annotations.label: Real normed division algebras

anchor.snippet: any real normed division algebra must be isomorphic to either the real numbers R , the complex numbers C , the quaternions H , or the octonions O

annotations.note: The Frobenius/Hurwitz classification of real normed division algebras is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quotient rule

anchor.snippet: The derivative of the quotient of two functions is given by the quotient rule

annotations.note: `deriv_div` (and `HasDerivAt.div`) state the quotient rule for derivatives.

annotations.label: Division by zero undefined

anchor.snippet: Division of any number by zero in most mathematical systems is undefined

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `a / 0 = 0` in any `GroupWithZero` (via `inv_zero`/`div_zero`) rather than treating it as undefined, so the Wikipedia 'undefined' statement is not literally formalized.
